title: The Intervention
subtitle: How to fix a system without breaking it, and what to do when someone else is breaking it on purpose

sections.content: You've identified the limiting factor. You understand the system's purpose. You know which modules are hegemonica and which are failing. Now you have to actually do something about it — and this is where most people cause more damage than the problem they were trying to solve.

Economic intervention is not complicated in theory. Find what's broken. Fix it. Measure whether it worked. The complication is that systems are made of interconnected parts, and touching one part sends vibrations through every other part, and some of those vibrations are invisible until they've already done their damage.
This article is about how to intervene without making things worse, and how to evaluate the results honestly. It's also about what to do when someone in the system is making things worse on purpose.
The Method

Rule one: focus on primary processes. Every system has processes that directly serve its purpose and processes that support those processes. The primary processes are the ones touching the customer, the product, the output — wherever the system's purpose meets reality. Start there, because that's where your measurements will be most accurate and your feedback most immediate.

Rule two: use practitioner knowledge — carefully. The people doing the work know things about the system that no spreadsheet captures. The machinist knows which machine drifts after two hours. The receptionist knows which clients are about to leave before management does. The delivery driver knows which route actually works versus which one the software suggests. This information has real value. It comes from proximity, not from databases.

But practitioners are also people, and people, when given the chance to be heard, will tell you about everything that bothers them — most of which has nothing to do with the limiting factor. The coffee machine is broken. The parking is unfair. Dave in logistics is annoying. These complaints are real and the person making them deserves to feel heard. Listen. Nod. Do not act on all of it.

Your job is to scrutinise each piece of practitioner information against the purpose of the system and the location of the limiting factor. Some of what they tell you will be gold — specific, actionable insight that no outsider could have. Some of it will be noise — legitimate grievances about non-crucial, non-limiting-factor issues that would consume your energy without moving the system forward. Let people feel heard. Then apply only what matters.

Rule three: measure what matters, not what's easy. Profit is a useful proxy for business performance, but it's one number summarising thousands of processes. A change in profit doesn't tell you which process changed or why. You need measures closer to the intervention — cycle time, defect rate, customer wait time, employee turnover in a specific department. The right measure depends on the specific module you're working on, and choosing the wrong measure is worse than not measuring at all because it gives you false confidence.

Rule four: respect what you don't understand. A system that's functioning has reasons for functioning, and not all of them are visible. The office birthday party that seems like a waste of time might be the only thing holding a fractured team together. The redundant approval step that slows everything down might exist because someone once shipped a catastrophic error without it. Before you remove a process you think is non-crucial, consider the possibility that you don't know why it's there — and the person who put it there might have known something you don't.
The Quadrant

Every intervention produces a result. That result falls somewhere on two axes: was it a success or a failure, and was the outcome expected or unexpected? These two axes create four quadrants, and each one tells you something different about your relationship with the system.

Fruition — success, expected. Your intervention achieved what you predicted. Your understanding of the system was sufficient. The measure improved. The limiting factor moved. This is the goal. It means your method worked, your practitioner information was good, and your measurement was accurate.

Serendipity — success, unexpected. Your intervention worked, but not in the way you predicted. Something positive happened that you didn't anticipate. This is a signal that you're interacting with the system in a way that exceeds your personal understanding — often because you're working with good practitioners whose knowledge goes deeper than yours. Serendipity means you've tapped into something. Pay attention. There's more value here than you planned for.

Mistake — failure, unexpected. Your intervention didn't work, and you didn't see it coming. This is the most common result for beginners and the most educational. It means your model of the system was wrong in some way — maybe the measure was wrong, maybe the practitioner information was incomplete, maybe the module you targeted wasn't actually the limiting factor. Mistakes cluster when you need to learn more about the system before intervening again. They are expensive, but they are honest.
Sabotage — failure, expected. Someone implemented something they knew would fail. This is the darkest quadrant, and it deserves its own section.
Reading Your Results
Over time, your interventions will form a pattern. Plot them on the quadrant — mentally or literally — and look at where they cluster.

Clustering in the top half means you're intervening well. Your understanding of the system is sound, your practitioner relationships are strong, and your measurements are accurate. If you're clustering in Fruition specifically, you're operating within your competence. If you're getting Serendipity, you're operating beyond it — in a good way — and the system is rewarding you for listening to the right people.

Clustering in Mistake means you need to slow down. You're intervening faster than you're learning. Go back to observation. Talk to more practitioners. Challenge your assumptions about which module is the limiting factor. The system is telling you that your model of it is incomplete.

You can use the quadrant to evaluate practitioners too. If you implement a suggestion from someone and it lands in Fruition or Serendipity, that person understands the system. Trust them with more. If their suggestions consistently land in Mistake, they may be well-intentioned but wrong — their model of the system is as incomplete as yours. Adjust accordingly.
The Saboteur

Sabotage is failure that was expected by the person who caused it. It's the implementation of a policy, process, or action that the implementer knows will fail — or at minimum, doesn't care if it fails.

Saboteurs are not always villains. Often they're employees who feel their abilities aren't being used. People who lost a negotiation and are punishing the system for it. People whose ego has been bruised and who have decided, consciously or not, that if they can't win, the system shouldn't either. Sometimes they don't even recognise what they're doing — they've rationalised their sabotage as a principled stand or a necessary correction.

There is a deeper variant. Some people resist change not because the specific intervention is wrong, but because the very idea of improvement challenges them on a fundamental level. If the system gets better, their excuses get worse. If processes improve, their underperformance becomes more visible. The existence of someone trying to make things work is, to these people, a personal accusation — even when it isn't directed at them at all.

These people don't just resist change passively. They sabotage the people attempting to improve the system. They feed you incorrect information. They undermine your interventions after you've implemented them. They create problems and position themselves as the solution. If you're clustering in the Sabotage quadrant and you can't figure out why, consider the possibility that someone in the system has identified you as a threat and is actively working to make you fail.

The pattern is recognisable: someone implements a change or performs a task in a way that they themselves would admit is suboptimal if pressed. The negative consequences land on others. When confronted, they redirect blame. They may claim the failure was unavoidable, or that the real problem is elsewhere, or that the person raising the issue is the actual problem.

Saboteurs usually emerge after a failed negotiation or a breakdown in communication. Someone felt unheard, disrespected, or overruled, and the sabotage is their response. Understanding this doesn't excuse it, but it does help you see it coming.
Dealing With It
The method is clinical. Don't make it personal. Don't make it emotional. Make it structural.

First: isolate responsibility. Make sure the policy or task is clearly owned by the person you suspect. No ambiguity about who decided what. This isn't about blame — it's about clarity. If responsibility is shared, the saboteur has cover. If it's isolated, the results are theirs.

Second: challenge publicly, on professional grounds. Not "you're doing this on purpose" — that's an accusation and it will escalate. Instead: "this process is producing inefficiency in these specific ways, and the data shows these specific costs." Frame it as a systems problem, not a people problem. Use the language of effectiveness, efficiency, and risk. The argument is against the process, not the person.

Third: let the dilemma do the work. A saboteur who has been publicly and professionally challenged on the performance of their process faces a choice. They can fix it — swapping from Sabotage to an attempt at Fruition — or they can double down, which risks their professional reputation in front of the people who just heard the data. If they have any ego at all, and saboteurs usually have plenty, they'll choose to fix it rather than be seen failing publicly.

Keep it professional. Keep it measured. Keep it about the system. The moment you make it personal, the saboteur gets exactly what they want: a conflict where they can play the victim. Don't give them that. Give them data and a choice.

And once they've been identified — once someone has made a direct, deliberate attempt to sabotage an intervention — reduce your exposure to them immediately. Do not give them a second chance to do it again. The likelihood that a person who has sabotaged you once will change their approach is extremely low. They have shown you who they are. Believe them. Engage only when necessary and with maximum caution. Your energy belongs on the system, not on managing someone who has decided the system shouldn't work.
The Loop
Intervene. Measure. Plot the result. Learn. Intervene again. This is the loop, and it never ends, because systems never stop changing and the limiting factor never stays in the same place for long.

When you remove a limiting factor, a new one appears — the next weakest link in the chain. The system improves, but improvement reveals the next constraint, and the next one, and the next one. This is not discouraging. This is the work. The system gets better each time you complete the loop, and your ability to complete the loop gets better each time you do it.
Plot your results. Watch where they cluster. Adjust your method. Trust the practitioners who earn your trust. Challenge the processes that deserve to be challenged. Leave the rest alone.
The system doesn't need you to fix everything. It needs you to fix the right thing, measure whether it worked, and have the discipline to stop when the answer is yes.
